paragraphs.text: As a concerned EU citizen and consumer of digital productsparticularly video gamesI would like to highlight an important issue that is missing from the current scope of the proposed Digital Fairness Act: the growing problem of digital games being remotely disabled after purchase. Recently, over one million EU citizens (including myself) supported the Stop Destroying Video Games European Citizens Initiative. It calls attention to the fact that many video games that we legally buy are being taken away without warning or recourse. This isnt just about minor games; some well-known titles, like The Crew by Ubisoft, have been made unplayableeven for people who paid full pricesimply because the publisher shut down the servers. The consumer is left with nothing. This situation is unacceptable. When we buy a game, we are not "renting" it. Yet that is effectively whats happening now: publishers reserve the right to shut down online features or even the whole game whenever they like, without offering any offline version, refund, or alternative. Most of us aren't even aware of this when we buy the game, buried in endless End User License Agreements (EULAs) that no one truly has the power to negotiate. The proposed Digital Fairness Act talks about improving transparency, stopping manipulative design, and making digital markets fairerand thats a great step forward. But fairness also means protecting what we buy from being deleted after the fact. The law must not overlook the massive consumer harm caused by the premature shutdown of digital games. Video games are not just entertainmentthey are art, culture, and social spaces. They deserve the same consumer protections we expect for films, music, or books. No one would accept it if a publisher could reach into your Kindle and delete a novel you purchased, or if a movie you bought on a streaming platform suddenly disappeared forever. Yet that is happening with gamesand worse, without legal safeguards in place. Therefore, I respectfully urge the Commission to include the following in the Digital Fairness Act: Require game publishers to provide a clear end-of-life plan when shutting down gameswhether through offline modes, legacy servers, or unlocking tools so communities can preserve the experience. Prohibit terms in EULAs that allow companies to remove access to purchased games without valid reason or compensation. These clauses are deeply unfair and exploit the imbalance between consumers and large digital platforms. Ensure national consumer agencies can enforce these rules and penalise companies that remove access to games without meeting these obligations. This isnt about demanding that companies keep servers running forever. Its about requiring transparency, alternatives, and respect for consumer rights when a game is no longer supported. Its a reasonable balance between business needs and digital ownership. I believe that updating our laws to reflect how people live and play today is essential. Millions of us now spend time, money, and creativity inside virtual worldsbuilding characters, stories, and communities. When a company deletes a game, it also erases those experiences. That should not be allowed to happen silently or without consequence. I hope the Commission listens to the message that over a million people have already voiced through the Stop Killing Games petition. Please include specific protections for digital games in the final version of the Digital Fairness Act. This is our chance to make digital fairness real. Thank you. Christopher Varszegi Ireland
